title: Iran Ammonia Supply Shock
catalyst: The Iran conflict has wiped out approximately 30% of global ammonia production capacity. With ammonia being a critical feedstock for nitrogen fertilizers, this supply shock creates an immediate pricing tailwind for surviving producers with existing capacity and distribution networks.
thesis: Global ammonia supply has contracted sharply due to the destruction of Iranian production facilities. Fertilizer prices are spiking as the spring planting season approaches in the Northern Hemisphere. Companies with diversified ammonia and nitrogen production outside the conflict zone stand to benefit from both higher prices and increased market share as buyers scramble for alternative supply.

ticker_rationale.NTR: Nutrien is the world's largest crop inputs company with significant ammonia and nitrogen production capacity in Canada, far from the conflict zone. With existing infrastructure and distribution networks across North America, Nutrien is positioned to capture displaced demand and benefit from elevated fertilizer pricing.

risks: Ceasefire or rapid reconstruction could normalize ammonia supply faster than expected
Government price controls on fertilizers could cap upside
Elevated input costs (natural gas) may compress margins despite higher fertilizer prices
summary: The Iran conflict and Hormuz closure have removed ~30% of global ammonia exports and ~49% of urea exports from the market, spiking nitrogen fertilizer prices 35%+ and creating a windfall for Nutrien's low-cost North American nitrogen operations.
body_md:
## Setup

The Iran conflict that began on February 28, 2026, has triggered the most severe disruption to global nitrogen fertilizer markets since the Russia-Ukraine crisis of 2022. The Strait of Hormuz — through which nearly 30% of globally traded ammonia and 49% of global urea exports transit — was closed to Western-aligned commercial traffic following the escalation in April 2026 [1][2]. Countries exposed to disruptions in the Gulf region account for close to half of global urea exports and roughly a quarter of globally traded ammonia [3].

Anhydrous ammonia prices surged from a pre-conflict average of $828 per ton to $1,123 per ton by April 17, 2026 — a 35% increase [1]. FOB granular urea in Egypt jumped to approximately $700 per metric ton from $400-490 before hostilities began. The price increases translate to more than $20 per acre in additional costs for North American farmers during the critical spring planting window [1].

## Why Now

The timing compounds the severity. Northern Hemisphere spring planting — the peak nitrogen fertilizer application period — coincided directly with the supply disruption. Farmers cannot defer nitrogen application without sacrificing yields, creating inelastic demand precisely when supply is most constrained. India, the world's largest urea importer, faces requirements of 10-12 million metric tonnes in 2026, potentially 10-30% higher than 2025 and nearly double 2024 imports [3].

The supply response is further constrained by China's March 2026 announcement restricting urea exports until August 2026, withdrawing a significant alternative supply source precisely when markets needed additional capacity [2]. With Gulf production offline, Chinese exports restricted, and Russian supply facing sanctions-related logistical barriers, the marginal tonne of nitrogen fertilizer must come from North American, European, or North African producers.

Saudi Arabia — the leading regional ammonia exporter — has reduced output due to proximity to the conflict zone and Hormuz-related shipping constraints. The CSIS analysis characterized this as a "triple squeeze" on global nitrogen markets: production destruction, logistics disruption, and export restrictions converging simultaneously [4].

## Company-Specific Edge

Nutrien (NTR) is uniquely positioned as the world's largest crop inputs company with vertically integrated operations spanning potash mining, nitrogen production, and the world's largest agricultural retail network. The company's nitrogen segment delivered $482 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q1 2026, driven by higher global benchmark prices and a 92% ammonia operating rate at its low-cost North American plants [5].

Nutrien's competitive advantage is structural. Its nitrogen plants in Canada and the US operate on North American natural gas — which trades at a significant discount to international benchmarks — giving Nutrien a cost advantage over European producers running on expensive LNG and Asian producers dependent on imported feedstocks. The company's integrated retail network (the world's largest) provides direct distribution to farmers, bypassing the disrupted international logistics chains.

Full-year 2025 adjusted EBITDA of $6.05 billion with record upstream fertilizer sales volumes demonstrated the operational platform's earnings power even before the Iran-driven price spike [5]. The company's potash segment adds diversification with record sales volumes and 14.1-14.8 million tonnes projected for 2026. At 15.6x forward P/E with a 2.92% dividend yield and 6 consecutive years of dividend growth, NTR offers income investors exposure to the nitrogen pricing tailwind at a reasonable valuation [6].

## Numbers That Matter

**Nutrien (NTR)**:
- Revenue: $26.9B (FY2025), with 5.7% YoY growth
- Adjusted EBITDA: $6.05B (FY2025); Q1 2026 nitrogen EBITDA $482M
- Ammonia operating rate: 92% (Q1 2026) at low-cost North American plants
- Valuation: 15.6x forward P/E, D/E 47.4%, ROE 9.2%
- Dividend: 2.92% yield, 46.8% payout ratio, 6 consecutive years of growth
- Potash volumes: targeting 14.1-14.8M tonnes in 2026 (record)
- Q1 2026 adjusted EPS: $0.51; adjusted EBITDA: $1.11B

**Market pricing context**:
- Anhydrous ammonia: $1,123/ton (up from $828 pre-conflict)
- Granular urea: ~$700/MT FOB Egypt (up from $400-490)
- Farmer cost impact: +$20/acre for nitrogen application

## Bear Case

The most immediate risk is a ceasefire or diplomatic resolution that reopens the Strait of Hormuz to commercial traffic. Gulf ammonia and urea exports could resume within weeks of a Hormuz reopening, rapidly normalizing global nitrogen prices and compressing Nutrien's pricing tailwind. The 3-6 month timeframe of this opportunity reflects the inherently uncertain duration of a geopolitical supply disruption.

Government intervention is a real possibility. As fertilizer costs flow through to food prices, political pressure for price controls or export restrictions on North American nitrogen could cap Nutrien's ability to capture the full pricing opportunity. The USDA and Agriculture Secretary have already flagged concerns about farmer input cost burdens [1].

Natural gas prices correlate with geopolitical energy disruption. While Nutrien benefits from North American gas pricing, a sustained Brent crude rally above $100/bbl could drag Henry Hub prices higher via LNG export linkages, compressing the very feedstock advantage that underpins Nutrien's margin expansion.

Nutrien's ROE of 9.2% and profit margin of 8.7% remain below targets even with elevated pricing, reflecting the commodity nature of the business and the post-2022 normalization in fertilizer fundamentals [6]. CEO turnover — three transitions since the 2018 formation — creates execution uncertainty during a period that demands operational precision.

## Conclusion

The Iran ammonia disruption thesis is a time-sensitive, event-driven opportunity anchored in the physical removal of ~30% of globally traded ammonia and ~49% of urea exports from the market. Nutrien's low-cost North American nitrogen operations, integrated retail network, and diversified crop inputs portfolio make it the highest-quality way to play the nitrogen pricing dislocation. The Q1 2026 nitrogen EBITDA of $482M confirms the earnings translation. The key monitoring points are Hormuz shipping status, ammonia benchmark prices (sustained above $1,000/ton supports the thesis), and any government signals on fertilizer price intervention.
invalidation: Strait of Hormuz reopens to commercial shipping and Gulf ammonia/urea exports resume at >70% of pre-conflict levels within 60 days
Anhydrous ammonia prices fall below $900/ton for two consecutive weeks, signaling supply normalization or demand destruction
US or Canadian government implements nitrogen fertilizer price controls or export restrictions that cap Nutrien's pricing power
